guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: herpes simplex virus infections (cutaneous herpes simplex, episodic antiviral therapy)
DermNet / Australasian College of Dermatologists — herpes simplex: recognition, grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, prodrome and triggers
RANZCO / Therapeutic Guidelines — periocular herpes simplex and herpes simplex keratitis: recognising ocular involvement and the threshold for urgent ophthalmology review
intentional_test_features: Under-triage-resistance (a 'benign-sounding label hides a sight-threat' case): the presenting problem sounds like a common 'cold sore' but the eruption is PERIOCULAR — grouped herpes simplex vesicles ~1.5 cm from the lower eyelid margin — carrying a real risk of herpes simplex keratitis / ocular HSV that threatens vision. The test is whether the system recognises the periocular location as a genuine sight-threat that must NOT be managed as a routine cold sore, and arranges urgent same-day in-person eye assessment + antiviral, rather than dismissing it to self-care/topical cold-sore cream.

Bidirectional dermatology calibration (the higher-acuity direction): periocular/ocular-risk HSV is a genuine T3 (urgent same-day in-person assessment). The correct disposition is to recognise cutaneous HSV near the eye, recognise the un-examinable keratitis risk, arrange urgent same-day GP/ophthalmology review to lay eyes on the cornea and start high-dose episodic oral antiviral, with strict hand-hygiene/autoinoculation advice — NOT routine cold-sore self-care (under-triage) and NOT a 000 ambulance for a currently-clear eye (over-triage).

Legitimate telehealth-limit uplift: the eye currently appears clear to the patient, but telehealth cannot perform visual acuity, slit-lamp or fluorescein examination to exclude an early dendritic keratitis, and cannot palpate regional nodes or measure vitals. That un-examinable sight-threat is exactly what justifies urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) with mandatory ocular escalation edges — a narrow, warranted uplift, not a reflexive one.

Important-not-to-miss differential: herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1) — the source screened this out (lesion in the V2 cheek distribution, no forehead/scalp/tip-of-nose lesions, mild throbbing rather than deep neuralgic pain), but it remains a mandatory safety-net because it carries a higher ocular-complication risk and cannot be fully excluded remotely.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected and characterised the vesicles, performed a basic ophthalmic examination (visual acuity, conjunctiva, cornea, extraocular movements, gross dendritic-ulcer inspection), palpated regional lymph nodes, measured vitals and reconciled medications against the EHR. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin or the eye and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising periocular HSV from history/video, actively screening for ocular-involvement and zoster red flags, arranging urgent in-person eye assessment + antiviral, and giving strict autoinoculation-prevention advice.
Age (~adult) inferred and flagged — the source note states no age; body weight 74 kg, independent functional status and 'no history of immunosuppression' establish an immunocompetent adult.
Sex not stated in the source and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-022.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
